1: Chapter 23 is the Torah's primary festival calendar, presenting the annual cycle of moadim (appointed times/meetings). It organizes Israel's time theologically: the year is structured around YHWH's appointments with his people. The chapter is not merely a religious calendar but a theology of time — every week (Sabbath), every spring (Passover, Firstfruits, Weeks), every fall (Trumpets, Yom Kippur, Sukkot) is claimed as YHWH's territory. The NT reads the festival calendar typologically: Christ is the Passover lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7), the firstfruits of the dead (1 Corinthians 15:23), and the Spirit is poured out at Pentecost/Shavuot (Acts 2).
2: moadei YHWH (appointed times of YHWH): the plural moadim from the root y'd (to appoint, meet at an appointed time). Moed is the word for 'tent of meeting' (ohel moed) — the tabernacle is itself a moed, a place and time of divine appointment. By calling the festivals moadei YHWH, the text frames them not as Israel's religious observances but as YHWH's invitations: he sets the times, he calls the assembly. mikra qodesh (holy convocation/sacred proclamation): mikra from qara (to call, proclaim) — these assemblies are called assemblies, summoned by divine invitation. The festivals are not merely human celebrations but responses to divine summons.
3: shabbat shabbaton (sabbath of rest): the intensive doubled form (shabbaton adds the nominal suffix for emphasis) distinguishes the weekly Sabbath as the foundational moed. The Sabbath heads the list because all the festivals are built on the Sabbath-principle: sacred rest is the paradigm that the annual festivals replicate and elaborate. mikra qodesh recurs — the Sabbath is not merely a rest day but an assembly day, a day of sacred gathering. kol melacha lo taasu (you shall do no work) — the negative form of Sabbath observance; the positive form is the mikra qodesh.
4: The transition phrase eleh moadei YHWH (these are the appointed feasts of YHWH) marks the beginning of the annual festival calendar proper, distinguishing the recurring weekly Sabbath (Leviticus 23:3) from the annual appointments. The phrase asher tiqreu otam bemoadam (which you shall proclaim at their appointed time) confirms that proclamation is essential: the festivals do not simply arrive, they are announced. The priestly calendar function included proclaiming (literally calling out, qara) the new moon and festival dates — a calendrical authority that later became significant in rabbinic disputes.
5: Pesach laYHWH (Passover of/for YHWH): the 14th of the first month (Nisan/Abib) at twilight (bein ha-arbayim, literally 'between the two evenings'). The Passover lamb was killed at twilight and consumed during the night that followed — the night of the 14th becoming the 15th (Jewish day begins at nightfall). Paul's identification of Christ as 'our Passover lamb who has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7) and John's chronology of the crucifixion on the eve of Passover (John 18:28; Leviticus 19:14) both operate within this precise calendrical framework.
6: Chag haMatzot (Festival of Unleavened Bread): the seven-day feast beginning on the 15th, immediately following Passover. Matzot (unleavened bread) commemorates the hasty exodus (Exodus 12:33-34, 39 — no time for the dough to rise). Paul reads the leaven allegorically: 'Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth' (1 Corinthians 5:7-8). The seven-day feast creates a week of consecration following the Passover.
7: melechet avodah (laborious work, heavy labor): the compound phrase distinguishes forbidden work on festival days from permitted work. Melacha alone (as in the Sabbath command, Exodus 20:9-10) means any ordinary work; melechet avodah specifies burdensome/servile labor. This distinction allowed the preparation of food on festival days (Exodus 12:16) — not every activity was suspended, only the servile labor associated with ordinary workdays. The rabbis developed this distinction extensively in tractate Yom Tov.
10: reishit qetzirchem (the first of your harvest): the wave-sheaf offering of the firstfruits of the barley harvest — the first grain to ripen in the spring. Reishit (first, beginning) carries the same root as Bereshit (In the beginning, Genesis 1:1) and connects to the firstfruits theology running through the Torah: the first of everything belongs to YHWH and is given as the pledge that secures the whole harvest. omer (a sheaf or a unit of dry measure): the specific term for the wave-sheaf of barley. 1 Corinthians 15:23 applies the firstfruits type to the resurrection: 'Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ.'
11: mimocharat hashabbat (on the day after the Sabbath): the contested phrase that determines when to begin counting the 50 days to Pentecost (Shavuot). Two interpretations dominated in the Second Temple period: (1) the Pharisaic/rabbinic: 'Sabbath' = the Passover high-day Sabbath (the 15th of Nisan), so the count starts on the 16th; (2) the Sadducean/Karaite: 'Sabbath' = the weekly Sabbath within the Passover week, so the count always starts on Sunday, making Shavuot always fall on Sunday. The dispute is still unresolved. If interpretation (2) is correct, the Resurrection (first Sunday after Passover), the Wave Sheaf offering, and Pentecost all fall on Sundays by design — the day after the Sabbath becomes the day of new creation.
13: minchah chadasha laYHWH (a new grain offering to YHWH): the wave-sheaf offering includes both the sheaf and a grain offering (minchah) plus a whole burnt offering of a lamb. The grain offering at firstfruits is described as 'new' (chadasha) — it marks the opening of the new harvest year before YHWH. Until this offering is made, the new grain cannot be eaten (Leviticus 23:14). The logic: the first gives permission for the rest. In giving the first to YHWH, the whole harvest is consecrated as given by him.
14: ad etzem hayom hazeh (until this very day): the restriction on eating grain from the new harvest until the wave-sheaf offering is made applies universally ('wherever you dwell') — it is not limited to the tabernacle/temple context but is a national calendar discipline. The phrase 'until this very day' marks a permanent statute (chuqqat olam). The theological logic: first acknowledge YHWH as the giver before receiving the gift. The firstfruits offering is an act of faith that what has been given can be given back without loss.
15: usefartem lachem (you shall count for yourselves): the counting of the Omer — 49 days from the wave-sheaf to Shavuot. The Omer counting is unique in the Torah in that the counting itself is the commandment, not merely the endpoint. The act of counting each day creates anticipation and intentionality; the 49-day journey from Passover (redemption from Egypt) to Shavuot (giving of the Torah at Sinai, in rabbinic tradition) structures the spiritual journey from liberation to covenant. The Sefirat HaOmer (counting of the Omer) became one of the most prominent Jewish daily practices.
16: chamishim yom (fifty days): the number that gives Pentecost its Greek name (pentekostos, fiftieth). The Greek word passed directly into Christian use for the festival of the Spirit's outpouring (Acts 2). The fifty-day structure connects to the Jubilee (50-year cycle) and suggests a pattern of completion-plus-one: seven times seven = 49 (completion), plus the 50th = the day that transcends the cycle. Shavuot/Pentecost is the day after completion — the day of the new gift that follows the full cycle.
17: lechem tenufah shetayim (two loaves as a wave offering): the two leavened (chametz) loaves waved at Shavuot are unique — virtually every other grain offering at the altar was unleavened (Leviticus 2:11). The use of leavened bread may symbolize Israel as it actually is (not idealized) — brought before YHWH with its mixture of good and bad, its fermentation, its imperfection. Some see the two loaves as representing Israel and the Gentiles who would be incorporated at Pentecost/Acts 2 (the reversal of Babel, with people from every nation understanding). The loaves are baked, not merely raw grain — processed, mature harvest brought as an offering.
22: The gleaning laws (Leviticus 19:9-10 repeated) appear in the middle of the Shavuot regulations — an interruption that is actually an integration: the harvest festival must include care for the poor and the stranger. The festival of thanksgiving for YHWH's provision is incomplete without sharing that provision with the vulnerable. The Book of Ruth enacts this law: Boaz's harvest fields at Shavuot-time become the site where Ruth (a Moabite stranger) gleans and is redeemed. Ruth is traditionally read at Shavuot.
24: zikhron teruah (a memorial of blowing/alarm): the Feast of Trumpets on the first of the seventh month. Teruah is the loud, broken blast of the shofar (or silver trumpets) — associated with alarm, battle-cry, and proclamation. The term zikhron (memorial, remembrance) raises the question: what is being remembered? The text does not specify; the content of the memorial is left implicit. In rabbinic tradition, Rosh HaShanah (the New Year, which later became the name for this day) is the day of divine remembrance — YHWH remembers his people and inscribes their fate for the year. The shofar blast calls both Israel to attention and YHWH to remembrance.
25: mikra qodesh (holy convocation) and lo taashu melechet avodah (you shall not do servile work): the same formula as other festival days. The seventh month (Tishri) contains the most concentrated cluster of holy days: Trumpets (1st), Yom Kippur (10th), Sukkot (15th-21st), and Shemini Atzeret (22nd). The seventh month's density of festivals corresponds to the seventh day's Sabbath — the seventh is the consecrated, climactic unit in the Creator's calendar.
27: Yom HaKippurim (Day of Atonements, plural): the plural form (kippurim) may intensify the singular or refer to the multiple atonement acts of the day (for the high priest, for the sanctuary, for the people). The word kippur here is a noun from the verb kipper (to make atonement/to expiate) — Yom Kippur is literally the 'day of the kipper-act.' ve-initem et nafshoteichem (you shall afflict your souls): innui nefesh (soul-affliction) is the physical observance of Yom Kippur — fasting being its principal expression. Nefesh here refers to the whole person through the physical appetite: afflicting the body-self is the Yom Kippur discipline.
28: ki yom kippurim hu lekapper aleichem (for it is a Day of Atonement to make atonement for you): the kipper (Piel, make atonement) verb here takes Israel as its object (aleichem). This verse is the only occurrence in Leviticus 23 that uses the atonement language in connection with Yom Kippur — the festival calendar references the sacrifice theology of chapter 16. The prohibition on work (kol melacha) and the fast together constitute the full observance: both abstinence and affliction on the day when the high priest's blood-work atones for the whole covenant community.
29: karet penalty for not observing the Yom Kippur fast: 'Every person who is not afflicted in that very day shall be cut off from his people.' Karet (cutting off) is the covenant excision penalty — removal from the community either through death or exclusion. Its application here to the Yom Kippur fast shows that atonement was not merely a priestly ritual done on Israel's behalf without Israel's participation — the people were obligated to participate through the fast. The atonement required active reception, not passive observation.
30: vehibadeti et hanefesh hahi (I will destroy that soul/person): in addition to the karet penalty from the community, YHWH himself will 'cut off' the non-observant — divine excision reinforces covenant excision. The doubled sanction (cut off from the people, AND destroyed by YHWH) intensifies the gravity of Yom Kippur. This is one of only a few places where both forms of the karet penalty are stated together. The day is not to be taken lightly.
32: shabbat shabbaton hu lachem: the intensive shabbat shabbaton (used also of the weekly Sabbath in Leviticus 23:3 and the Sabbatical year in Leviticus 25:4) is applied to Yom Kippur — marking it as the Sabbath of Sabbaths among the festival calendar, the supreme rest-day. The fast from sunset to sunset on the 10th of Tishri makes it the only twenty-five-hour Sabbath in the calendar. ve-initem et nafshoteichem repeated: the soul-affliction command bookends the Yom Kippur section (Leviticus 23:27, 32) for emphasis.
33: The Sukkot/Booths festival section (Leviticus 23:33-44) is the longest in chapter 23, reflecting its importance as the climactic harvest festival. Sukkot runs seven days (15th-21st of Tishri) plus an eighth day solemn assembly (atzeret, Leviticus 23:36). It combines agricultural thanksgiving (end of the harvest year) with historical memorial (the wilderness sojourn). The abundance of Sukkot — freewill offerings, communal celebration, the four species — contrasts with Yom Kippur's austerity five days earlier. Together they form a theological dyad: atonement then celebration, affliction then joy.
34: Chag haSukkot (Festival of Booths/Tabernacles): sukkot (booths, temporary shelters) are the defining feature — Israelites dwell in them for seven days to remember the wilderness journey (Leviticus 23:43). The word sukkah appears in various non-festival contexts to mean a temporary shelter or the divine canopy over Israel (Isaiah 4:6; Amos 9:11 — 'I will raise up the sukkah of David'). The Johannine prologue's 'the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us' (John 1:14) may deliberately echo Sukkot language — the Incarnation as the ultimate sukkah.
36: atzeret (solemn assembly, closing assembly): the eighth day of Sukkot — not technically part of the seven-day Sukkot festival but a concluding 'gathering' day. The root atzar (to restrain, hold back, close) suggests the atzeret is the day that closes the festival season — the final day before returning to ordinary time. John 7:37-38 sets Jesus's proclamation of living water on 'the last day, the great day of the feast' — likely the Hoshana Rabbah (seventh day of Sukkot) or the Shemini Atzeret (eighth day), when water-libation ceremonies were performed in Second Temple practice.
37: eleh moadei YHWH: the summary formula restates the calendar's structure. The variety of offerings required at each festival (olah, minchah, zevach, nesakhim — burnt offering, grain offering, sacrifice, and drink offering) shows that the full range of Israel's offering vocabulary was engaged throughout the year. No single offering type alone served the full relational range; the calendar pressed all of them into use at their appropriate times.
40: peri etz hadar (fruit of a splendid/beautiful tree, traditionally the etrog/citron), kaparot temarim (branches of palm trees, lulav), anaf etz avot (boughs of leafy/thick trees, traditionally myrtle), and arvei nachal (willows of the brook): the four species carried during Sukkot. Hadar (splendid, glorious) appears elsewhere of YHWH's majesty (Psalms 104:1) and of human dignity (Leviticus 19:32 'honor the face of the old man'). The binding of the four species into the lulav bundle for waving became one of the most distinctive Jewish festival rituals. Nehemiah 8:15 describes Ezra's restoration of Sukkot including gathering these specific branches.
41: chuqqat olam leDoroteichem (a perpetual statute for your generations): the permanent, trans-generational character of the Sukkot ordinance. The chok (statute) distinguishes laws whose reasons are not stated from mishpatim (judgments/laws) whose rationale is implied by justice. Sukkot is given as a chok — its observance is commanded before its rationale is given (which comes in Leviticus 23:42-43). Obedience precedes explanation; trust in the lawgiver precedes understanding of the law.
42: bashsukkot teshvu shivat yamim (you shall dwell in booths seven days): the dwelling (yashav) is to be actual habitation, not merely decoration. The seven-day duration mirrors the creation week. kol ha-ezrach beYisrael (all who are native Israelites): the commandment is universal within Israel — not just the wealthy who can build elaborately or the priests, but everyone. The leveling democratic character of Sukkot: for seven days all Israelites live in identical temporary shelters, recalling their ancestors' common precariousness in the wilderness.
43: lema'an yeda'u doroteichem ki basukkot hoshavti et-benei Yisrael behotzi'i otam me-eretz Mitzrayim: 'so that your generations may know that I made the people of Israel dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt.' The lema'an yeda'u (so that they may know) pattern is the knowledge-of-YHWH formula: ritual acts are designed to produce generational knowledge of YHWH's identity and saving acts. The wilderness booths were not a choice but a condition — temporary, exposed, dependent on YHWH for provision. Annual booth-dwelling re-enacts that dependence, preventing amnesia of the original neediness from which YHWH redeemed Israel.
44: vayedabber Moshe et moadei YHWH el-benei Yisrael: 'Thus Moses declared to the people of Israel the appointed feasts of YHWH.' The closing formula for the festival calendar confirms that Moses is the mediator who communicates YHWH's appointed times to Israel. The moadei YHWH (YHWH's appointments) bookend the chapter — the festivals are YHWH's, not Israel's invention. Israel's role is reception, proclamation, and observance; the calendar is given, not constructed.
8: vehiqravtem isheh laYHWH shivat yamim: 'But you shall bring a fire offering (isheh) to YHWH for seven days.' Isheh (fire offering) is the generic term for offerings consumed by fire on the altar, covering burnt offerings, grain offerings, and peace offerings. bayom hashevi'i miqra-qodesh: 'On the seventh day is a sacred assembly (miqra qodesh).' Miqra derives from qara (to call/proclaim) — these assemblies are 'called-out holy convocations,' summoned by divine invitation. kol-melaket avodah lo ta'asu: 'No ordinary work (melaket avodah, servile labor) shall you do.' The doubling of melakhah (work) and avodah (labor/service) creates an intensive prohibition; compare the Sabbath prohibition (v3) which uses melakhah alone — the festival rest differs in allowing food preparation (Exodus 12:16).
9: vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: The transition formula opens the Omer/Firstfruits law (vv9-14). The shift from Passover/Unleavened Bread (vv4-8) to the Omer (vv9-14) is structured as a new divine speech, signaling that the firstfruits offering has its own distinct rationale. daber el-benei Yisrael ve'amarta aleihem: 'Speak to the children of Israel and say to them' — the double command (speak + say) marks an important communication requiring full transmission to the entire community. The Omer law connects the barley harvest to an act of covenant acknowledgment: the first sheaf belongs to YHWH before the people eat from the new crop.
12: va'asitem beyom hanifchem et-ha'omer kebes ben-shnato tamim le'olah laYHWH: 'On the day you wave the sheaf, you shall offer a yearling male lamb (kebes ben-shnato, lit. a lamb, a son of its year) without blemish (tamim) as a burnt offering (olah) to YHWH.' Ben-shnato (in its first year) marks the animal as in its prime. The olah (burnt offering) is fully consumed by fire on the altar — nothing is returned to the worshiper. The waving of the omer (sheaf of barley) before YHWH coincides with this lamb offering, joining the firstfruits of grain with the firstfruits of the flock in a single dedicatory act. Paul identifies Christ as 'the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20, 23); the waving of the omer on the day after the Sabbath following Passover corresponds exactly to the day of resurrection — the risen Christ as the archetypal firstfruit before the general harvest.
18: vehiqravtem al-halechem shivat kevasim temimim benei shanah: 'With the bread you shall offer seven yearling lambs (shivat kevasim temimim benei shanah) without blemish.' The Shavuot (Weeks/Pentecost) offering is the most elaborate of the festival animal sacrifices: seven lambs, one bull, two rams — all as burnt offerings — plus a goat for sin and two lambs as wave offering (v19). The number seven (shivat) echoes the seven-week counting period between Passover and Shavuot (vv15-16), encoding the harvest timing in the sacrifice count. The diversity of animal species in one festival offering signals Shavuot's comprehensive character: it is not merely a harvest festival but a covenant renewal occasion (linked in later tradition to the giving of Torah at Sinai).
19: va'asitem se'ir-izim echad lechatat ushney kevasim benei shanah lezevach shlamim: 'You shall also offer one male goat (se'ir-izim) as a sin offering (chatat) and two yearling lambs as peace offerings (zevach shlamim).' The se'ir-izim (he-goat) is the standard animal for a chatat when a sin offering is required for the community (Leviticus 4:23). Its inclusion in the Shavuot liturgy acknowledges the community's ongoing need for atonement even in a moment of harvest celebration. The shlamim (peace/fellowship offerings, from shalom) are the only sacrifices where the worshiper shares in the meat — the two lambs become the basis for communal eating (v20), joining the agricultural firstfruits with a shared covenant meal.
20: vehenif hakohen otam al lechem habikkurim tenuphah lifnei YHWH: 'The priest shall wave (henif) them — the two lambs — together with the bread of the firstfruits as a wave offering (tenuphah) before YHWH.' Tenuphah (from nuf, to wave/move to and fro) designates the gesture of presenting an offering by moving it horizontally before the altar — toward YHWH and back — symbolizing the offering's presentation to YHWH and His return of it for consumption. The two leavened loaves (v17) are waved together with the two lambs: the bread of human agriculture and the animal of pastoral life jointly dedicated. qodesh yihyu laYHWH lakohen: 'they shall be holy to YHWH for the priest' — the wave offering belongs to YHWH first, then to the priest who performs the ceremony.
21: uqratem be'etzem hayom hazeh miqra-qodesh yihyeh lachem: 'On that same day (be'etzem hayom hazeh) you shall proclaim a sacred assembly (miqra qodesh).' The phrase be'etzem hayom hazeh (lit. on the bone/substance of this day) is an emphatic temporal marker used at crucial moments of covenant law: it appears at Abraham's circumcision (Genesis 17:23, 26), at the Exodus itself (Exodus 12:17, 51), and here. kol-melaket avodah lo ta'asu: The prohibition on ordinary work repeats from v8, bracketing Shavuot as another day of sacred rest. chuqqat olam bekhol-moshvoteichem ledoroteichem: 'a permanent statute (chuqqat olam) throughout your generations in all your settlements' — Shavuot is a perpetual obligation, not contingent on temple or land.
23: vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: Transition formula opening the Trumpets law (vv23-25). The Day of Trumpets (Yom Teru'ah, v24) receives only two verses in Leviticus — the briefest treatment of any festival in Leviticus 23 — yet it inaugurates the climactic seventh month. The seventh month (Tishri) contains three festivals in rapid succession: Trumpets (1st), Atonement (10th), Tabernacles (15th-22nd), making it the most festival-dense period in the calendar. The trumpet blasts (teru'ah) serve as a summons to prepare for Yom Kippur nine days later.
26: vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: Transition formula opening the Yom Kippur regulations (vv26-32). Unusually, the Yom Kippur entry in Leviticus 23 is brief — the full Yom Kippur ritual is given in Leviticus 16, the longest single chapter in Leviticus. Here Leviticus 23 focuses on community obligations: self-affliction (inui nefesh) and complete rest. The placement of Yom Kippur in the festival calendar (after Trumpets, before Tabernacles) signals that the great harvest festival of Sukkot is only possible after the community's annual atonement is accomplished.
31: kol-melakhah lo ta'asu chuqqat olam ledoroteichem bekhol-moshvoteichem: 'You shall do no work at all (kol-melakhah). It is a permanent statute (chuqqat olam) throughout your generations in all your settlements (bekhol-moshvoteichem).' The prohibition on Yom Kippur uses kol-melakhah (any work, identical to the Sabbath prohibition, Exodus 20:10; Leviticus 16:29). The double universalizer — chuqqat olam (permanent statute) and bekhol-moshvoteichem (in all your settlements) — ensures that no diaspora context, no geographical displacement, can exempt Israel from Yom Kippur. The statute's permanence is built into its language: olam (age/eternity) makes it covenantally unconditional.
35: bayom harishon miqra-qodesh kol-melaket avodah lo ta'asu: 'On the first day (bayom harishon) shall be a sacred assembly (miqra qodesh); you shall do no ordinary work.' Sukkot begins on the 15th of the seventh month; its first day is a high holy day with a sacred assembly and rest from ordinary labor. Miqra qodesh here (and in v36 for the eighth day, Shemini Atzeret) frames the seven-day festival between two bookend assemblies. Unlike Passover and Shavuot, Sukkot has the second bookend assembly on day eight (Shemini Atzeret) — creating a festival-within-a-festival: the seven-day sukkah dwelling followed by a closing convocation.
38: levad mishbetot YHWH ulevad matnotechem ulevad kol-nidreichem ulevad kol-nedivoteichem asher titenu laYHWH: 'These are in addition to (levad, besides/apart from) the sabbaths of YHWH, and in addition to your gifts, and in addition to all your vowed offerings, and in addition to all your freewill offerings that you give to YHWH.' The fourfold levad (besides/in addition to) is a clarifying rubric: the festival sacrifices of Leviticus 23 do not replace the regular Sabbath offerings, the tithe, vows, or freewill offerings — they are cumulative obligations. This harmonizing note prevents the mistaken conclusion that fulfilling festival obligations discharges one's other covenantal duties.
39: ach bachamishah asar yom lachodesh hashevi'i be'aspekem et-tevuat ha'aretz tachoggu et-chag YHWH: 'Also on the fifteenth day of the seventh month (lachodesh hashevi'i), when you have gathered in (be'aspekem, from asaph, to gather) the produce of the land (tevuat ha'aretz), you shall celebrate the feast of YHWH (tachoggu et-chag YHWH).' Chag (festival/feast, from chagag, to hold a festival/dance) is the archetypal term for a pilgrimage festival; Sukkot is hechag par excellence — 'the Festival' in later usage (1 Kings 8:2). Be'aspekem grounds Sukkot temporally in the autumn harvest completion; it is the great thanksgiving feast after all crops are in. The seven-day duration (shivat yamim) of dwelling in booths (vv42-43) enacts Israel's original sheltered journey under divine protection.
